title: Problem 1
statement_text: Does every graph $G$ admit a pivot Gray code on its spanning trees?
context_text: A pivot Gray code for spanning trees requires that consecutive spanning trees differ by an edge exchange sharing a common endpoint (the strong revolving door property). Although the corresponding reconfiguration graph is connected for any graph $G$, it remains open whether all graphs admit such a code. Pivot Gray codes have been constructed for fan graphs and more generally for outerplanar graphs.
paper_title: A Gray code for arborescences of tournaments

title: Problem 2 (Knuth)
statement_text: Does every directed graph $G$ admit a pivot Gray code on its arborescences rooted in a given vertex?
context_text: This problem was proposed by Knuth as Exercise 7.2.1.6–102 in The Art of Computer Programming with an estimated difficulty of 46/50. Gray codes on rooted arborescences naturally satisfy the strong revolving door property, so a positive answer would immediately imply a positive answer to Problem 1 on pivot Gray codes for spanning trees of undirected graphs.
paper_contribution: Proves a positive answer for tournaments: for any tournament $G$ and vertex $r$, the flip graph of the arborescences of $G$ rooted in $r$ admits a Hamiltonian path (Theorem 1). Also characterises cases where an arborescence has degree 1 in the flip graph and constructs directed graphs whose flip graph is an unbalanced bipartite graph (hence has no Hamiltonian cycle), showing the general question remains open.
paper_title: A Gray code for arborescences of tournaments
